summary:
Child mortality rates are determined using an age range encompassing infants through just before their fifth birthday; data emphasize high-risk periods starting at birth and extending until around four or five years old. This comprehensive window ensures accurate representation across all significant developmental phases where risks increase sharply but then decrease significantly thereafter. To summarize concisely while focusing only on essential information related directly to determining the correct answer based on available knowledge about child mortality metrics,

Child mortality rates typically cover ages from newborns up to approximately 4–5 years, reflecting heightened vulnerability especially within those first few crucial postnatal years when many fatal outcomes occur such as infections, malnutrition, injuries etc., making Options involving similar ranges highly plausible given current global health surveillance practices aimed precisely towards tracking survival trends amongst younger populations vulnerable beyond mere infant stage alone. Thus confidently selecting **Option A** encapsulates appropriate temporal boundaries needed per standard protocols utilized internationally today regarding pediatric public health indicators measuring
